source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.topic: Weather, daylight and change across the four seasons
source_alignment.source_statement: Observe changes across the four seasons; observe and describe weather associated with the seasons and how day length varies.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack builds longitudinal observation rather than fixed season stereotypes. Learners compare dated observations, describe weather as variable, notice daylight patterns and use safe local evidence without being asked to experience uncomfortable weather.

objective.child_goal: I can notice and describe how weather, plants and daylight change through the year.
objective.statement: Observe and compare seasonal changes, describe weather using simple evidence, and recognise that daylight length changes across the year.
misconceptions: Treats each season as having one fixed kind of weather, such as winter always meaning snow.
Uses one day's weather to decide the season without checking other evidence.
Thinks all trees lose their leaves in autumn or that leaf loss means a tree is dead.
Thinks longer daylight means the Sun is always visible or the weather must be warm.
Confuses daily weather changes with the slower pattern of seasonal change.

vocabulary.child_explanation: One part of the year: spring, summer, autumn or winter.
vocabulary.audio_script: The year has four seasons: spring, summer, autumn and winter.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Four dated panels around one yearly circle.

vocabulary.child_explanation: What the air and sky are like now, such as sunny, cloudy, rainy or windy.
vocabulary.audio_script: Weather tells us what it is like outside now.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Simple sun, cloud, rain and wind observation icons.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Look, listen or notice carefully and record what you find.
vocabulary.audio_script: Observe means notice carefully before you decide.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A child-safe window view, listening icon and picture record card.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The light outside between sunrise and sunset.
vocabulary.audio_script: Daylight is the part of the day when it is light outside.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two equal clock strips showing different light and dark lengths.

vocabulary.audio_script: A change is something becoming different over time.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: The same local tree shown on four dated observation cards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Listen and look. Choose one weather word that matches the evidence.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise describing current weather without inferring a season.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Notice the sky, rain and wind clues. We are describing today, not guessing the whole season.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: One calm window scene with optional cloud, rain and wind indicators.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Meet the four seasons around the year wheel.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce seasons as repeating parts of the year rather than four weather labels.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Spring, summer, autumn and winter come in a repeating order. Many things can change as the year goes round.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Four labelled, dated local-observation panels arranged in a circle.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: What changed on this tree between the two visits?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model comparison of the same place or organism at different times.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: In the first picture the branches have many leaves. Later, fewer leaves remain. We compare what we can see.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The same deciduous tree in two clearly dated photographs or reviewed illustrations.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort each observation into weather, plant change or daylight.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Separate kinds of evidence and avoid deciding from one stereotyped clue.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Ask: is this evidence about today's weather, a plant changing, or how long it stays light?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three audio-labelled evidence trays with one observation card at a time.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which day has more daylight? Does that tell us today's weather?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check daylight comparison and distinguish it from temperature or cloud.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Compare the light parts of the day strips. Daylight length does not tell us whether today is sunny or rainy.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two equal day strips with longer and shorter daylight sections plus separate weather cards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Pip says, 'It cannot be winter because there is no snow.' Teach Pip what evidence to check.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair fixed-weather season stereotypes through multi-clue reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I looked for snow only. What other observations could help me?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A mild winter observation card, date marker, bare deciduous tree and short-daylight strip.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare the same safe local scene across dates and record weather, plant and daylight observations.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Children tap picture symbols, select spoken labels, point for an adult or use partner-assisted scanning; date and location remain fixed for fair comparison.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Indoor window or reviewed-image mode replaces outdoor exposure; no requirement to tolerate rain, wind, bright sun, cold, mud or strong smells.

manipulatives.purpose: Describe weather from observable clues while keeping it separate from season identification.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: One evidence card is sorted at a time; all labels are spoken and uncertain mixed-weather observations can accept more than one accurate descriptor.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: No sound effect is essential, wind is also shown with static indicators, and symbol descriptions support blind or low-vision adult mediation.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare relative daylight duration using matched day strips rather than requiring clock calculation.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Children align two strips and choose longer, shorter or about the same; sunrise and sunset words are available as optional extension audio.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: High-contrast patterns and text labels supplement light/dark colour; no flashing sunrise animation or precise time reading is required.

practice.interleaving_notes: Begin with observation language and matched-scene comparisons. Interleave season labels only after children can distinguish current weather, longer-term plant change and daylight evidence.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Describe current weather from clear observable evidence without assigning a season.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: reviewed sky scene + 1-3 weather clues + spoken descriptor choices + optional more-than-one answer
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid implying weather categories are exclusive; audio and still-image evidence must agree.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare repeated observations of the same local place across the year.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same scene + two or four dated views + plant, clothing-independent and ground/sky clues + what-changed prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Reject scenery where camera angle or art style creates the apparent change; do not use clothing as sole season evidence.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Sequence and compare age-appropriate plant changes while distinguishing deciduous and evergreen examples.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: reviewed plant or tree sequence + spoken observation + order or same/different response
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not imply every plant follows one timetable; use healthy examples and avoid declaring dormant plants dead.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare day length across seasons and separate daylight evidence from daily weather.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: matched 24-hour strips + longer/shorter/about-same choice + separate weather card check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use relative comparison, not causal claims or exact astronomical explanation; patterns must not depend on colour alone.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Retain four-season order and choose multiple observations that support a seasonal comparison.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: spaced queue + spoken claim + 3-5 evidence cards + select one or two useful observations + explain or point
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include mild winters, rainy summers and mixed days so no single weather stereotype becomes the rule.

body.prompt: It is raining on a July observation day. Which statement is scientifically careful?
choices: It cannot be summer.
Summer days can be rainy.
Rain means winter.
body.supported_interaction: select and place cards using taps, numbered positions or step controls; dragging is optional. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: Summer days can be rainy.

hints: Weather can change from day to day.
A season can have different kinds of weather.
question_variants.explanation: Rain can happen in any season, so one rainy day does not rule out summer.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "Summer days can be rainy.". Rain can happen in any season, so one rainy day does not rule out summer.
feedback.repair: Weather can change from day to day. A season can have different kinds of weather. Use that clue to repair weather equals season, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this weather sort, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "Summer days can be rainy.": Rain can happen in any season, so one rainy day does not rule out summer.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: What changed on the same tree between the two dated pictures?
choices: leaf amount and colour
the tree became a different object
nothing changed
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: leaf amount and colour

hints: Look at the branches in both pictures.
Compare the leaves you can see.
question_variants.explanation: The same tree has fewer leaves and the visible leaf colour has changed.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "leaf amount and colour". The same tree has fewer leaves and the visible leaf colour has changed.
feedback.repair: Look at the branches in both pictures. Compare the leaves you can see. Use that clue to repair daily weather vs seasonal change, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this season observe, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "leaf amount and colour": The same tree has fewer leaves and the visible leaf colour has changed.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: This evergreen tree still has green needles in winter. Is that possible?
choices: yes
no
body.supported_interaction: select and place cards using taps, numbered positions or step controls; dragging is optional. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: yes

hints: Not every tree loses all its leaves.
Evergreen trees keep green leaves or needles through the year.
question_variants.explanation: Evergreen trees can keep green leaves or needles in winter.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "yes". Evergreen trees can keep green leaves or needles in winter.
feedback.repair: Not every tree loses all its leaves. Evergreen trees keep green leaves or needles through the year. Use that clue to repair all trees lose leaves, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this picture sequence, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "yes": Evergreen trees can keep green leaves or needles in winter.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: Which strip shows more daylight?
choices: A
B
same
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: B

hints: Compare only the light parts.
The longer light section means more daylight.
question_variants.explanation: Strip B has a longer light section, so it represents more daylight.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "B". Strip B has a longer light section, so it represents more daylight.
feedback.repair: Compare only the light parts. The longer light section means more daylight. Use that clue to repair daylight equals weather, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this daylight compare, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "B": Strip B has a longer light section, so it represents more daylight.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats each season as having one fixed kind of weather, such as winter always meaning snow.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child rejects a season because a stereotyped weather feature is absent.
repair_sequence: Keep the date and season label visible.
Compare two different weather days within that season.
Choose other seasonal evidence while saying weather can vary.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child accepts a non-stereotypical weather day within a named season.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses one day's weather to decide the season without checking other evidence.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child labels a warm winter day as summer or a cool summer day as winter.
repair_sequence: Add date, repeated plant observations and daylight comparison.
Decide which clues show weather now and which show change across the year.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child distinguishes weather evidence from seasonal evidence in a new example.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks all trees lose their leaves in autumn or that leaf loss means a tree is dead.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child says an evergreen must lose its needles or identifies a healthy bare deciduous tree as dead.
repair_sequence: Compare a reviewed evergreen and deciduous tree across dates.
Name only visible changes and avoid guessing health from leaf cover alone.
Sort a fresh tree sequence by what stayed the same and what changed.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child recognises that tree types can show different seasonal leaf patterns.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks longer daylight means the Sun is always visible or the weather must be warm.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child uses sunny or hot as the definition of a longer day.
repair_sequence: Compare daylight strips without weather pictures.
Add cloudy and rainy cards to both long and short days.
State that daylight duration and weather are different observations.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child identifies the longer-day strip despite a cloudy or rainy weather card.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Confuses daily weather changes with the slower pattern of seasonal change.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child describes yesterday-to-today rain change as a new season.
repair_sequence: Place daily weather cards on a short timeline.
Place monthly scene observations on a longer timeline.
Describe which changes can happen quickly and which are noticed across many visits.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child classifies a new observation as daily weather or longer-term seasonal change.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use still scenes, no background weather audio by default, gentle narration and no simulated lightning, thunder or gust effects.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace season-wheel turns, moving clouds and daylight transitions with static dated panels and outlined differences.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Use produced, human-listened narration for every observation, date cue, weather word and comparison; replay never reduces evidence.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use clear scenes, weather symbols and spoken labels; dates are adult-readable but not required for child decoding.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Show one observation card at a time and use the repeated notice-record-compare routine.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow long inspection time, zoomed stills, side-by-side comparison, repeated audio and adult-described images.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit accurate weather description even when season reasoning needs repair and avoid presenting uncertain real-world weather as a trick.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach season names, weather, sunny, cloudy, rainy, windy, leaf, change, daylight, longer and shorter using pictures and gestures.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires accurate weather description, one matched-scene seasonal comparison and relative daylight comparison. Secure requires rejecting a fixed-weather stereotype and selecting more than one useful seasonal clue after spaced review.
evidence.parent_summary: Can describe weather, compare the same place across the year and notice that plant appearance and daylight can change with the seasons.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Stores observed evidence separately from inferred season, response mode and reading demand, including whether the learner transfers to non-stereotypical weather examples.
next_actions: Build a repeated class observation record from the same safe viewpoint.
Route weather stereotypes to within-season contrast examples.
Compare a reviewed evergreen and deciduous local tree.
Revisit daylight patterns across longer spaced intervals.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 1 seasonal-changes pack with a deterministic 220-item pilot bank. Four curated variants are preserved alongside candidates covering all four seasons, current weather, relative daylight, plant and tree changes, selected animal observations, same-place comparisons, evidence selection, misconception repair and simple longitudinal records. Generated claims distinguish a single day's weather from season, keep animal and plant findings tied to the observed sample, and use cautious language for natural variation. Every item offers visual, tactile-symbol, text and supported response routes without requiring outdoor or sensory exposure. Optional audio references are ElevenLabs-only, unavailable pending human listening review, and never fall back to browser TTS. Playful field missions remain untimed and reward noticing and comparing rather than speed. Independent science, geography/date context, image, SEND, safeguarding, narration and renderer review remains required before promotion.
